news_summary: Advancements in AI are accelerating the discovery of room temperature superconductors, fueling innovation in computer chip technology and expanding practical access to versatile rental robots.
news_summary_fr: Les progrès de l'intelligence artificielle accélèrent la découverte de supraconducteurs à température ambiante, stimulent l'innovation dans le domaine des puces informatiques et élargissent l'accès pratique à des robots de location polyvalents.
news_summary_es: Los avances en inteligencia artificial están acelerando el descubrimiento de superconductores a temperatura ambiente, impulsando la innovación en la tecnología de los chips informáticos y ampliando el acceso práctico a robots de alquiler versátiles.

articles.title: AI just supercharged the race to find room temperature superconductors
articles.summary: Scientists have combined machine learning with quantum physics to discover two new superconductors and create a much faster way to search for many more. The technique could bring researchers significantly closer to the long-sought goal of a room-temperature superconductor.
articles.content:
Machine learning is giving scientists a powerful new way to search for superconductors, materials that conduct electricity with zero resistance. An international team has demonstrated that AI can rapidly narrow an almost limitless number of possible material combinations to identify the most promising candidates. According to Aalto University Professor Päivi Törmä, who leads the SuperC consortium, the approach could dramatically speed the discovery of new superconductors.

Superconductors allow electric current to flow without losing energy, but only when cooled to extremely low temperatures where quantum effects emerge. These remarkable materials are already used in technologies ranging from quantum computers and medical neuroimaging systems to fusion reactors and maglev trains.

Despite their enormous potential, superconductors remain exceptionally difficult to discover. There are virtually endless combinations of chemical elements that could form new materials, yet only a tiny fraction turn out to be superconductors. Those that have already been identified generally require costly cooling systems that bring them close to absolute zero before they exhibit their unique properties.

Scientists around the world are searching for a practical superconductor that can operate at room temperature.

"Superconductive materials that can operate at room temperature would forever change the way we consume energy," explains Törmä. "If such a material could replace regular conductors in applications like computers and data centers, global energy consumption could be slashed and the heat footprint of the ICT sector vastly reduced."

AI and Quantum Physics Join Forces

The SuperC consortium was established in 2023 by Professor Törmä and an international group of leading physicists who share the goal of using quantum physics to help address climate change. It is the first coordinated global collaboration dedicated to discovering new superconductors, with the ambitious objective of finding a room temperature superconductor by 2033.

According to Törmä, combining quantum geometry with machine learning provides a powerful foundation for that search. In the team's latest work, the newly identified superconductors, YRu 3 B 2 and LuRu 3 B 2 , owe their properties to electrons forming flat bands within a kagome lattice, a geometric arrangement inspired by traditional Japanese basket weaving patterns.

To identify these materials, researchers first used machine learning to rapidly screen enormous numbers of possible elemental combinations. A specialized algorithm selected the most promising candidates, which were then analyzed using detailed quantum calculations to determine whether they could become superconductors.

Once the predictions were confirmed theoretically, collaborators at Rice University synthesized the materials by chemically combining their constituent elements into new compounds. Led by Professor Emilia Morosan, the Rice team then experimentally verified that both materials are indeed superconductors.

The proof of concept study was recently published in Physical Review Research.

A Faster Path to New Superconductors

Developing a complete quantum mechanical understanding of superconductivity is extraordinarily challenging, making the search for new superconducting materials slow and computationally demanding.

"Over the decades researchers have recognized over 7,000 superconductors, but mostly serendipitously," explains Törmä. "The process of identifying possible materials is so computationally heavy that, in fact, researchers have only been able to theoretically predict the viability of about 20 of these."

Even when a material appears promising on paper, it may still prove impractical because it is too difficult to synthesize or impossible to produce at scale, Törmä notes. Traditionally, evaluating huge numbers of potential materials has required enormous computing resources. The SuperC team's AI driven approach changes that process by focusing detailed calculations only on the strongest candidates.

"Our method uses machine-learning-based pre-screening followed by targeted calculations on the promising candidates. This approach will greatly speed up superconductor discovery in the future. With machine learning, we may be able to push the number of materials we can process into the billions," says Törmä. "This will take us a critical step closer to finding a room-temperature superconductor."

Looking Ahead

SuperC's research will be featured in Aalto University's Designs for a Cooler Planet exhibition from September 1 to October 30, 2026, in Greater Helsinki, Finland.

The SuperC consortium receives funding from The Kavli Foundation, Klaus Tschira Stiftung, Kevin Wells, the Jane and Aatos Erkko Foundation, the Keele Foundation, the Magnus Ehrnrooth Foundation, and the Neste and Fortum Foundation.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough where AI combined with quantum physics accelerates the discovery of new superconductors, potentially leading to room-temperature superconductors. This advancement could drastically reduce global energy consumption and transform multiple technologies, representing a broad and meaningful impact. The article provides detailed context about the discovery process, the consortium involved, and the potential benefits, fulfilling the criteria for inspiring good news.
articles.personality_title: AI and quantum physics speed up discovery of new superconductors
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Superconductors are special materials that let electricity flow without any resistance, but they usually only work at very cold temperatures. Scientists want to find superconductors that work at room temperature because they could save a lot of energy and improve many technologies.

**What happened** – An international team of scientists led by Professor Päivi Törmä at Aalto University used artificial intelligence (AI) combined with quantum physics to find two new superconductors named YRu3B2 and LuRu3B2. They first used AI to quickly test many possible material combinations, then used detailed quantum calculations to check the best ones. A team at Rice University then made these materials and confirmed they are superconductors.

**Impact** – This new method is much faster than traditional ways of finding superconductors, which often take a long time and a lot of computer power. By using AI to focus only on the most promising materials, researchers can now explore billions of possibilities more quickly. Finding superconductors that work at room temperature could reduce energy waste in computers, data centers, and other devices.

**What's next step** – The team aims to find a superconductor that works at room temperature by 2033. Their work will be shown at an exhibition in Helsinki, Finland, in 2026. The research is supported by several foundations and continues to push the boundaries of material discovery.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Combining AI with quantum physics lets scientists discover superconductors faster, bringing us closer to materials that could save energy by working at room temperature.
articles.personality_title_fr: L'IA et la physique quantique accélèrent la découverte de nouveaux supraconducteurs
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les supraconducteurs sont des matériaux spéciaux qui laissent passer l'électricité sans résistance, mais ils fonctionnent généralement à des températures très basses. Les scientifiques cherchent à trouver des supraconducteurs qui fonctionnent à température ambiante car cela pourrait économiser beaucoup d'énergie et améliorer de nombreuses technologies.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Une équipe internationale dirigée par la professeure Päivi Törmä de l'Université Aalto a utilisé l'intelligence artificielle (IA) combinée à la physique quantique pour découvrir deux nouveaux supraconducteurs appelés YRu3B2 et LuRu3B2. Ils ont d'abord utilisé l'IA pour tester rapidement de nombreuses combinaisons de matériaux possibles, puis des calculs quantiques détaillés pour vérifier les meilleurs candidats. Une équipe de l'Université Rice a ensuite fabriqué ces matériaux et confirmé qu'ils sont supraconducteurs.

**Impact** – Cette nouvelle méthode est beaucoup plus rapide que les méthodes traditionnelles, qui prennent souvent beaucoup de temps et de ressources informatiques. En utilisant l'IA pour se concentrer uniquement sur les matériaux les plus prometteurs, les chercheurs peuvent désormais explorer des milliards de possibilités plus rapidement. Trouver des supraconducteurs fonctionnant à température ambiante pourrait réduire le gaspillage d'énergie dans les ordinateurs, les centres de données et d'autres appareils.

**Prochaine étape** – L'équipe vise à trouver un supraconducteur fonctionnant à température ambiante d'ici 2033. Leur travail sera présenté lors d'une exposition à Helsinki, en Finlande, en 2026. La recherche est soutenue par plusieurs fondations et continue de repousser les limites de la découverte de matériaux.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Combiner l'IA avec la physique quantique permet aux scientifiques de découvrir plus rapidement des supraconducteurs, nous rapprochant de matériaux capables d'économiser de l'énergie en fonctionnant à température ambiante.
articles.personality_title_es: La IA y la física cuántica aceleran el descubrimiento de nuevos superconductores
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los superconductores son materiales especiales que permiten que la electricidad fluya sin resistencia, pero normalmente solo funcionan a temperaturas muy bajas. Los científicos quieren encontrar superconductores que funcionen a temperatura ambiente porque podrían ahorrar mucha energía y mejorar muchas tecnologías.

**Qué pasó** – Un equipo internacional de científicos dirigido por la profesora Päivi Törmä de la Universidad Aalto utilizó inteligencia artificial (IA) combinada con física cuántica para encontrar dos nuevos superconductores llamados YRu3B2 y LuRu3B2. Primero usaron IA para probar rápidamente muchas combinaciones posibles de materiales, luego usaron cálculos cuánticos detallados para revisar las mejores opciones. Un equipo en la Universidad Rice fabricó estos materiales y confirmó que son superconductores.

**Impacto** – Este nuevo método es mucho más rápido que las formas tradicionales de encontrar superconductores, que suelen tomar mucho tiempo y usar mucha potencia de computación. Al usar IA para enfocarse solo en los materiales más prometedores, los investigadores ahora pueden explorar miles de millones de posibilidades más rápido. Encontrar superconductores que funcionen a temperatura ambiente podría reducir el desperdicio de energía en computadoras, centros de datos y otros dispositivos.

**Próximo paso** – El equipo busca encontrar un superconductor que funcione a temperatura ambiente para 2033. Su trabajo se mostrará en una exposición en Helsinki, Finlandia, en 2026. La investigación cuenta con el apoyo de varias fundaciones y continúa avanzando en el descubrimiento de materiales.

**Resumen en una frase** – Combinar IA con física cuántica permite a los científicos descubrir superconductores más rápido, acercándonos a materiales que podrían ahorrar energía funcionando a temperatura ambiente.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a glowing kagome lattice pattern woven like delicate Japanese basketry, with softly illuminated electrons flowing smoothly along its interconnected lines, surrounded by abstract representations of AI neural networks gently guiding the lattice, all rendered in natural, muted earth tones against a serene, softly glowing background symbolizing breakthrough and harmony in scientific discovery.

articles.title: The rise of computer chips — and the race to control them
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 06 July 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-02099-yAn analysis sets out the history of computing beyond software and Californian entrepreneurs.
articles.content:
The Chip Age: How Chips Shaped Our Past and Will Define Our Future Rakesh Kumar Icon (2026)

Few items are so imperceptible to the public, yet so essential to their lives, as the semiconductor. The computer chip powers the machinery, systems and interfaces that people interact with on a daily basis. The average person will encounter a semiconductor dozens of times a day, whether it’s a small chip in their thermostat, in their computer or phone’s motherboard or in their vehicle’s entertainment system.

Despite their diminutive status, chips have become a very big deal. They are the object through which almost every modern anxiety passes: artificial intelligence, industrial sovereignty, military escalation, environmental strain, supply-chain fragility and the future of scientific discovery.

How wealthy tech entrepreneurs seek to shape politics, culture and the future — and why we must resist

Rakesh Kumar, a computer engineer at the University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign, understands how important chips are. And in The Chip Age, he tries to explain how these fingernail-sized gizmos have become the material substrate of contemporary power.

At its best, the book is a reminder that the history of computing is not just the history of software, entrepreneurs and Californian myth-making. It is a history of materials, manufacturing, state subsidy, trade battles and small technical decisions that later change the world.

Kumar outlines the geological accident 380 million years ago that made the town of Spruce Pine, North Carolina, the world’s main source of the high-purity quartz needed for chip-manufacturing crucibles. And he describes the fierce 1980s trade battles, tariffs and lawsuits against the ‘dumping’ of goods into domestic markets between the United States and Japan that ultimately led to the decline of the Japanese memory-chip industry.

Industrial history

The account is particularly strong when describing the early history of chip integration, when electronics moved from discrete components to circuits etched on a single substrate. Kumar writes that institutions, not lone geniuses, pushed forward the development of chips. However, he notes the breakthroughs of engineer Jack Kilby at Texas Instruments in Dallas and physicist Robert Noyce at Fairchild Semiconductor: Kilby invented the first integrated circuit on a single block of germanium in 1958, and Noyce subsequently worked out how to build a mass-producible chip that integrated all of the components and their interconnections onto a single block of silicon.

Breakthrough computer-chip tech could help meet ‘monumental demand’ driven by AI

Kumar’s account of Acorn Computers, the BBC Micro and the eventual birth of UK-based chip titan Arm, is wonderful. In the late 1970s, the UK broadcaster the BBC launched a computer-literacy project, and the computing firm Acorn won the contract to build the BBC Micro, which became a hit in UK schools and homes. When planning their next computer, Acorn found that the existing microprocessors were inadequate and partnered with US electronics company VLSI Technology in 1983 to design their own chip: the Acorn RISC Machine. This highly efficient chip caught the attention of US technology giant Apple for its Newton handheld device. To manage this collaboration, Acorn spun off its 12-person processor division in 1990 to form Advanced RISC Machines (now Arm), which pioneered the revolutionary and wildly successful model of licensing its chip designs rather than manufacturing them.

Similarly, Kumar’s sections on Japan’s rise to become a leading producer of memory chips, the 1986 Japan–United States Semiconductor Agreement, South Korea’s ascent through the dynamic random-access memory market and Taiwan’s foundry model are larded with reflections on today’s chip hierarchy, showing how the current global race was confected through policy choices, industrial bets and ruthless trade pressure.

Concentrated market

Explaining semiconductors to a lay audience is no mean feat, and Kumar does so admirably. The book is full of details that make a reader sit up: Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company in Hsinchu produces more than half of the world’s chips and more than 90% of the most advanced ones. Globally, more than one trillion chips are shipped every year, with a total value of almost US$700 billion, and annual sales could exceed $1 trillion by 2030.

Kumar also gives a sense of scale to the state subsidy race to ensure chip supremacy: $52 billion from the United States, €43 billion ($48 billion) from Europe, $24.5 billion from Japan, $10 billion from India and more than $100 billion each from South Korea and China.

Staff wear protective suits to inspect semiconductor wafers at a facility in Taiwan.Credit: Billy H.C. Kwok/Bloomberg/Getty

He also goes beyond the usual geopolitical debate and addresses the issues of environmental damage, labour, rare-earth minerals, counterfeit components and the geopolitical quandaries that world leaders face.
articles.reasoning: The article provides a detailed, substantive analysis of the history and global significance of semiconductor chips, highlighting their essential role in modern technology and the broad geopolitical, economic, and environmental impacts of the semiconductor industry. It discusses breakthroughs, industrial evolution, and large-scale state investments, which have meaningful benefits for society and technology advancement.
articles.personality_title: How computer chips quietly shape our world and future
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Computer chips, also called semiconductors, are tiny parts inside many devices like phones, cars, and computers. Although people rarely notice them, these chips are very important because they control how these devices work.

**What happened** – Rakesh Kumar, a computer engineer, wrote a book called The Chip Age. It explains how small chips have become powerful tools shaping technology, politics, and economies worldwide. The book tells stories about important moments, like early inventions in the 1950s, how countries like Japan and Taiwan became leaders in chip-making, and the rise of companies like Arm, which designs chips used by Apple and others.

**Impact** – The book shows that chips are more than just technology; they affect global trade, government policies, and the environment. For example, Taiwan makes most of the world’s advanced chips, and countries are investing billions of dollars to lead in chip production. The story helps us understand how these tiny parts influence big issues like artificial intelligence and international relations.

**What's next step** – With huge investments from countries like the US, Europe, China, and India, chip technology will keep growing. This may lead to new inventions and stronger industries but also challenges like environmental impact and supply risks. Understanding this history helps people see why supporting chip research and fair trade is important.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Computer chips are small but powerful parts that have shaped our technology and world, and learning their story helps us prepare for the future.
articles.personality_title_fr: Comment les puces informatiques façonnent discrètement notre monde et notre avenir
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les puces informatiques, appelées semi-conducteurs, sont de petites pièces présentes dans de nombreux appareils comme les téléphones, les voitures et les ordinateurs. Bien que peu visibles, ces puces sont très importantes car elles contrôlent le fonctionnement de ces appareils.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Rakesh Kumar, ingénieur en informatique, a écrit un livre intitulé The Chip Age. Il explique comment ces petites puces sont devenues des outils puissants qui influencent la technologie, la politique et l’économie dans le monde entier. Le livre raconte des moments clés, comme les premières inventions dans les années 1950, comment des pays comme le Japon et Taïwan sont devenus leaders dans la fabrication de puces, et l’essor d’entreprises comme Arm, qui conçoit des puces utilisées par Apple et d’autres.

**Impact** – Le livre montre que les puces sont plus que de la technologie ; elles influencent le commerce mondial, les politiques gouvernementales et l’environnement. Par exemple, Taïwan fabrique la majorité des puces avancées, et les pays investissent des milliards pour être leaders dans ce domaine. Cette histoire aide à comprendre comment ces petites pièces touchent des sujets importants comme l’intelligence artificielle et les relations internationales.

**Étape suivante** – Avec d’énormes investissements de pays comme les États-Unis, l’Europe, la Chine et l’Inde, la technologie des puces va continuer à évoluer. Cela pourrait entraîner de nouvelles inventions et renforcer les industries, tout en posant des défis comme l’impact environnemental et les risques d’approvisionnement. Comprendre cette histoire aide à voir pourquoi il est important de soutenir la recherche sur les puces et un commerce équitable.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Les puces informatiques sont de petites pièces puissantes qui ont façonné notre technologie et notre monde, et connaître leur histoire nous prépare pour l’avenir.
articles.personality_title_es: Cómo los chips informáticos moldean silenciosamente nuestro mundo y futuro
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los chips informáticos, también llamados semiconductores, son partes pequeñas que están dentro de muchos dispositivos como teléfonos, autos y computadoras. Aunque la mayoría de la gente no los nota, estos chips son muy importantes porque controlan cómo funcionan estos aparatos.

**Qué pasó** – Rakesh Kumar, un ingeniero informático, escribió un libro llamado The Chip Age. Explica cómo estos pequeños chips se han convertido en herramientas poderosas que influyen en la tecnología, la política y la economía mundial. El libro cuenta momentos importantes, como los primeros inventos en los años 1950, cómo países como Japón y Taiwán se convirtieron en líderes en la fabricación de chips, y el surgimiento de empresas como Arm, que diseña chips usados por Apple y otros.

**Impacto** – El libro muestra que los chips son más que tecnología; afectan el comercio global, las políticas de los gobiernos y el medio ambiente. Por ejemplo, Taiwán produce la mayoría de los chips avanzados, y países están invirtiendo miles de millones para ser líderes en la producción de chips. Esta historia ayuda a entender cómo estas pequeñas partes afectan temas grandes como la inteligencia artificial y las relaciones internacionales.

**Próximo paso** – Con grandes inversiones de países como Estados Unidos, Europa, China e India, la tecnología de chips seguirá creciendo. Esto puede traer nuevos inventos y fortalecer las industrias, pero también desafíos como el impacto ambiental y riesgos en el suministro. Conocer esta historia ayuda a entender por qué es importante apoyar la investigación y el comercio justo en chips.

**Una frase clave** – Los chips informáticos son partes pequeñas pero poderosas que han moldeado nuestra tecnología y mundo, y conocer su historia nos prepara para el futuro.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a glowing circuit board shaped like a globe, with delicate quartz crystals growing from its surface and stylized, interconnected chip patterns radiating outward, set against a soft, natural color palette that evokes the fusion of technology, global industry, and the foundational materials powering the modern world.

articles.title: Robots available for rent: But what can they do?
articles.summary: Robotics tech is changing fast, so for many it makes sense to rent a robot.
articles.content:
In hospitals across the US, patients and staff have become accustomed to seeing a one-armed, four-foot high, friendly-looking white robot going about its business.

Nurses have been known to greet Moxi, as the robot is called by its maker Diligent Robotics, with a "good morning", a high five or even a hug.

Moxi – which shuttles medical supplies around hospitals – might respond by displaying its heart-shaped LED eyes and a beep beep greeting of its own.

"We get a lot of feedback that Moxi feels like a part of the team," says Todd Brugger, chief operating officer at the Texas-based robotics company, which has around 100 of the wheeled robots in operation.

But bringing Moxi into a hospital doesn't mean buying one of the machines outright. Instead, it is among robots available to rent or on a subscription basis.

Robotics companies use the term robotics-as-a-service. As well as the robot itself, service, maintenance and upgrades are bundled into the deal. A human engineer sitting in a remote control room may be on hand to take control of the robot if needed.

In Moxi's case, Brugger says: "It lowers the expense and the outlay for the hospital because you're not paying for the full purchase up front. Secondly, and I think more importantly, this tech is evolving very quickly… we're routinely evolving the software and capabilities of the robot."

Robot rentals are becoming available for anything from a day to years for a variety of purposes, from Moxi's hospital deliveries to robot bartenders or autonomous weeders for farms.

Increasingly this includes early humanoid models, designed to behave and look like humans, and operate in environments designed for people.

Given humanoids are still a work-in-progress, they are currently rented out for clearly defined tasks. That often means entertainment. Depending on the model, a machine might dance, sing or serve guests at a wedding or corporate event.

Ethan Qi, a Beijing-based associate director at Counterpoint Research, says an act like a humanoid dance routine is relatively simple to pull off.

"You hire a real dancer to perform and video it. The video is then used to train the robot. Then the robot will know how to dance. But the engineer will still often go with the robot in case the environment or the platform isn't simple," he says.
articles.reasoning: The article highlights the positive real-world impact of robotics-as-a-service, particularly in hospitals where robots like Moxi assist medical staff by delivering supplies, improving efficiency, and integrating well into human teams. The rental model lowers costs and allows rapid technological updates, making advanced robotics more accessible and beneficial to society. The article provides sufficient detail on the technology's application, scope, and evolving nature.
articles.personality_title: Hospitals and events rent robots to help with tasks and entertain
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Robots are becoming more common in places like hospitals and events, but buying one can be expensive. To make them easier to use, companies now offer robots for rent or through subscriptions, including service and upgrades.

**What happened** – In US hospitals, a robot named Moxi helps by delivering medical supplies. Nurses even greet it like a teammate. Instead of buying Moxi, hospitals rent it, which lowers costs and lets them get software updates quickly. Other robots are rented too, like humanoid robots that can dance or serve guests at parties.

**Impact** – Renting robots makes advanced technology affordable and easy to update. It helps hospital staff by saving time and fits smoothly into their work. For events, humanoid robots can entertain guests with dances or songs, showing how robots can do simple but useful jobs.

**What's next step** – As technology improves, more types of robots will be available to rent for different tasks. Engineers will keep improving how robots work and interact with people, making them even more helpful in everyday life.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Renting robots like Moxi lets hospitals and other places use smart machines affordably while keeping them up-to-date and useful.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des robots à louer pour aider les hôpitaux et divertir lors d'événements
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les robots deviennent plus courants dans des lieux comme les hôpitaux et les événements, mais en acheter un peut coûter cher. Pour faciliter leur utilisation, des entreprises proposent désormais des robots à louer ou en abonnement, avec service et mises à jour inclus.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Dans les hôpitaux américains, un robot nommé Moxi aide en livrant des fournitures médicales. Les infirmières le saluent comme un membre de l’équipe. Au lieu d’acheter Moxi, les hôpitaux le louent, ce qui réduit les coûts et permet des mises à jour rapides du logiciel. D’autres robots sont aussi loués, comme des humanoïdes qui dansent ou servent lors de fêtes.

**Impact** – La location rend la technologie avancée abordable et facile à mettre à jour. Elle aide le personnel hospitalier en gagnant du temps et s’intègre bien dans leur travail. Pour les événements, les robots humanoïdes peuvent divertir les invités avec des danses ou des chansons, montrant que les robots peuvent accomplir des tâches simples mais utiles.

**Prochaine étape** – Avec les progrès technologiques, davantage de types de robots seront disponibles à la location pour différentes tâches. Les ingénieurs continueront d’améliorer leur fonctionnement et leur interaction avec les humains, les rendant encore plus utiles au quotidien.

**Phrase clé** – Louer des robots comme Moxi permet aux hôpitaux et autres lieux d’utiliser des machines intelligentes à moindre coût tout en les maintenant à jour et utiles.
articles.personality_title_es: Robots para alquilar ayudan en hospitales y animan eventos
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los robots son cada vez más comunes en lugares como hospitales y eventos, pero comprarlos puede ser caro. Para facilitar su uso, las empresas ahora ofrecen robots en alquiler o por suscripción, incluyendo servicio y actualizaciones.

**Qué pasó** – En hospitales de EE. UU., un robot llamado Moxi ayuda entregando suministros médicos. Las enfermeras lo saludan como a un compañero. En lugar de comprar a Moxi, los hospitales lo alquilan, lo que reduce costos y permite actualizaciones rápidas del software. También se alquilan otros robots, como humanoides que pueden bailar o atender en fiestas.

**Impacto** – Alquilar robots hace que la tecnología avanzada sea asequible y fácil de actualizar. Ayuda al personal hospitalario a ahorrar tiempo y se integra bien en su trabajo. En eventos, los robots humanoides pueden entretener a los invitados con bailes o canciones, mostrando que los robots pueden realizar tareas simples pero útiles.

**Próximo paso** – A medida que la tecnología mejore, habrá más tipos de robots para alquilar en diferentes tareas. Los ingenieros seguirán mejorando cómo funcionan los robots y cómo interactúan con las personas, haciéndolos aún más útiles en la vida diaria.

**Frase clave** – Alquilar robots como Moxi permite a hospitales y otros lugares usar máquinas inteligentes de forma económica mientras se mantienen actualizadas y útiles.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a friendly, one-armed white robot with heart-shaped glowing eyes gently carrying medical supplies through a softly lit hospital corridor, surrounded by subtle silhouettes of nurses offering high fives and hugs, all rendered in natural, muted tones to evoke a sense of teamwork and care.
